items.mistake: Maximizing L(θ) directly instead of ℓ(θ) on many data points
items.fix: Work with log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = Σ log P(xᵢ | θ); argmax L = argmax ℓ, but ℓ avoids numerical underflow and simplifies derivatives.
items.why: Multiplying thousands of probabilities in (0,1) underflows to 0 in floating point; sums of logs remain representable.
items.example: 1000 Bernoulli trials: L(p) = p^k(1−p)^(1000−k) can round to 0; ℓ(p) = k log p + (1000−k) log(1−p) stays finite.

items.mistake: Solving ∇ℓ(θ) = 0 without checking it is a maximum
items.fix: Verify the Hessian is negative definite at the critical point, or compare ℓ at boundaries and multiple critical points.
items.why: Critical points can be minima or saddles; MLE requires the global maximum of ℓ over the valid parameter space.
items.example: A quadratic in μ has one critical point; but constrained p ∈ [0,1] may maximize ℓ at p = 0 or p = 1 instead of an interior root.

items.mistake: Treating minimization of an arbitrary loss as MLE without naming the model
items.fix: State the assumed P(x | θ) or P(y | x, θ); show that your loss equals −ℓ(θ) under that model.
items.why: Not every loss is an NLL; calling everything MLE hides model mismatch and wrong uncertainty assumptions.
items.example: Huber loss is robust regression, not Gaussian NLL; MSE is Gaussian NLL for the mean with fixed variance.

items.mistake: Using n−1 in Gaussian variance when the goal is MLE
items.fix: MLE of σ² divides by n: σ̂² = (1/n)Σ(xᵢ−μ)²; use n−1 for unbiased sample variance, not for MLE.
items.why: Confusing estimation goals changes the objective and breaks equivalence to argmax ℓ(σ²).
items.example: For n = 5, deviations squared sum to 20; MLE gives σ̂² = 4.0, unbiased estimator gives 5.0.
